using-imports-and-helper-files.label: Building a Multi-File Python App
using-imports-and-helper-files.summary: Create module files inside a folder, import your own functions into main.py, and connect the pieces into a small app.

sketch0.title: Your app can have more than one file

sketch1.title: Import from a file inside a folder

q1.title: Why split code into files?
q1.prompt: Why would a Python project put reusable functions in files such as `tools/names.py` instead of putting every line in `main.py`?
q1.hint: Think about keeping the main app readable and reusing the same logic later.

help.concept: A module file can hold reusable functions. `main.py` can stay focused on the app flow: read input, call functions, and print results.
help.hint_1: Look for the option about organization and reuse.
help.hint_2: The goal is not to make more work. The goal is to keep each file responsible for a clear job.

options.a: It keeps reusable code organized and lets `main.py` import it
options.b: It makes Python ignore errors in the imported file
options.c: It means the project can only run with one input value
options.d: It prevents functions from returning values

q2.title: Choose the direct import
q2.prompt: You created `tools/names.py` with a function named `clean_name`. Which line imports that function into `main.py`?
q2.hint: The path uses the folder name, then the file name without `.py`, then the function name.

help.concept: To import from a module inside a folder, use `from folder.file import function`. For `tools/names.py`, the module path is `tools.names`.
help.hint_1: Do not include `.py` in the import line.
help.hint_2: The correct form starts with `from tools.names import ...`.

q3.title: Good module responsibilities
q3.prompt: Choose all examples that are good jobs for a small module file in this lesson.
q3.hint: Pick jobs that are reusable and focused.

help.concept: Good module files usually hold related functions, such as text cleanup, formatting, scoring, or report building.
help.hint_1: A module should have a clear purpose.
help.hint_2: Avoid choices that put unrelated random code into one file.

options.b: `tools/badges.py` builds badge text from a name and role
options.c: `tools/random_everything.py` mixes unrelated code with no clear job
options.d: `main.py` must copy every function from every module before it can run

q4.title: Valid imported calls
q4.prompt: Which calls are valid after the matching import? Choose all correct answers.
q4.hint: Match the call style to the import style.

help.concept: After `from tools.names import clean_name`, call `clean_name(...)` directly. After `import tools.badges as badges`, call `badges.make_badge(...)`.
help.hint_1: Direct imports use the function name directly.
help.hint_2: Alias imports use the alias before the dot.

options.a: After `from tools.names import clean_name`, use `clean_name(" ava ")`
options.c: After `from tools.names import clean_name`, use `tools.names.clean_name("Ava")` without importing `tools`

q5.title: Order the module workflow
q5.prompt: Put these steps in the order you would use when adding your own module file to a small app.
q5.hint: Create the file before importing from it.

help.concept: Python can import a module only after the file exists in the expected folder path. Then `main.py` can call functions from that file.
help.hint_1: The folder and file come before the import.
help.hint_2: The function call happens after the import line.

tokens.t2: Create a module file such as `tools/names.py`
tokens.t3: Define a function inside that module file
tokens.t4: Import and call the function from `main.py`

q6.title: Build the import flow
q6.prompt: Arrange these lines so `main.py` imports a formatter and prints the returned result.
q6.hint: Import first, then call, then print.

help.concept: A main file usually imports the function it needs, calls it with data, and prints or stores the returned value.
help.hint_1: The import line must run before the function call.
help.hint_2: Print the stored message after the function returns it.

q7.title: Complete the function import
q7.prompt: Fill in the missing function name to import it from `tools.names`.
q7.hint: This is the function that cleans extra spaces and capitalization.

help.concept: `from tools.names import clean_name` imports the `clean_name` function from the `names.py` file inside the `tools` folder.
help.hint_1: The missing word is the function name, not the folder name.
help.hint_2: Choose the name that describes cleaning a name.

q8.title: Complete the module path
q8.prompt: Fill in the missing folder name in this import line.
q8.hint: The module files for this project live inside one folder.

help.concept: The import path `tools.badges` means Python should look for `badges.py` inside the `tools` folder.
help.hint_1: Do not type `.py` in the import path.

q9.title: Create a name-cleaning module
q9.prompt:
This project is starting a small helper package. Create a `tools` folder, add `tools/names.py`, and define `clean_name(text)` there. Then import `clean_name` from `tools.names` in `main.py`, clean the input name, and print it.

For example, if the input is `aVA`, your program should print:
`Ava`.
q9.hint: The required path is `tools/names.py`. The import line in `main.py` should be `from tools.names import clean_name`.

help.concept: Each project step is cumulative, so the existing files remain part of the solution while one new requirement is added.
help.hint_1: Retain the existing files and methods while completing the next responsibility.
help.hint_2: Test the normal path and inspect the final output before completing the step.

q9.starterCode:
# Create a folder named tools.
# Inside it, create tools/names.py.
# Define clean_name(text) in that file.

raw_name = input()

# Import clean_name from tools.names.
# Print the cleaned name.

main_py.content:
from tools.names import clean_name

raw_name = input()
print(clean_name(raw_name))

tools_init_py.content: # Tools package for this mini app.

q9.solutionCode:
from tools.names import clean_name

raw_name = input()
print(clean_name(raw_name))

q10.title: Add a badge module
q10.prompt:
The helper folder needs one more tool. Create `tools/badges.py`, import `make_badge` from `tools.badges`, clean the input name, build the badge text, and print it.

For example, if the inputs are `lina` and `artist`, your program should print:
`ARTIST badge: Lina`.
q10.hint: Create `tools/badges.py`, then import with `from tools.badges import make_badge`.

help.concept: Each project step is cumulative, so the existing files remain part of the solution while one new requirement is added.
help.hint_1: Preserve the previous milestone, changing only what the new feature requires.
help.hint_2: Confirm the program reaches the expected behavior before asking the grader to evaluate it.

q10.starterCode:
from tools.names import clean_name

raw_name = input()
role = input()

# Create tools/badges.py.
# Import make_badge from tools.badges.
# Clean the name, build the badge, and print it.

tools_init_py.content: # Tools package for this mini app.

main_py.content:
from tools.names import clean_name
from tools.badges import make_badge

raw_name = input()
role = input()
name = clean_name(raw_name)
print(make_badge(name, role))

tools_init_py.content: # Tools package for this mini app.

tools_badges_py.content:
def make_badge(name, role):
    return f"{role.upper()} badge: {name}"

q10.solutionCode:
from tools.names import clean_name
from tools.badges import make_badge

raw_name = input()
role = input()
name = clean_name(raw_name)
print(make_badge(name, role))

q11.title: Build a mission report module
q11.prompt:
Now connect the helper modules into one final report. Create `tools/reports.py`, import `mission_report` from `tools.reports`, and print the finished mission report.

For example, if the inputs are `zoe`, `scout`, and `18`, your program should print:
`Mission report: SCOUT badge: Zoe has 18 points`.
q11.hint: This step combines three files: `tools/names.py`, `tools/badges.py`, and your new `tools/reports.py`.

help.concept: Build on the supplied working version instead of restarting the project from an empty file.
help.hint_1: The earlier behavior remains part of the contract, so add the new logic without removing it.
help.hint_2: Test the normal path and inspect the final output before completing the step.

q11.starterCode:
from tools.names import clean_name
from tools.badges import make_badge

raw_name = input()
role = input()
points = int(input())

# Create tools/reports.py.
# Import mission_report from tools.reports.
# Print the final mission report.

tools_init_py.content: # Tools package for this mini app.

tools_badges_py.content:
def make_badge(name, role):
    return f"{role.upper()} badge: {name}"

main_py.content:
from tools.names import clean_name
from tools.badges import make_badge
from tools.reports import mission_report

raw_name = input()
role = input()
points = int(input())
name = clean_name(raw_name)
badge = make_badge(name, role)
print(mission_report(badge, points))

tools_init_py.content: # Tools package for this mini app.

tools_badges_py.content:
def make_badge(name, role):
    return f"{role.upper()} badge: {name}"

tools_reports_py.content:
def mission_report(badge, points):
    return f"Mission report: {badge} has {points} points"

q11.solutionCode:
from tools.names import clean_name
from tools.badges import make_badge
from tools.reports import mission_report

raw_name = input()
role = input()
points = int(input())
name = clean_name(raw_name)
badge = make_badge(name, role)
print(mission_report(badge, points))

try-using-imports-and-helper-files-check.title: Import from a helper file
try-using-imports-and-helper-files-check.prompt:
Build this as a small two-file program.

1. Create `tools/names.py`.
2. In `tools/names.py`, define `clean_name(text)`.
3. In `main.py`, import `clean_name` with `from tools.names import clean_name`.
4. Read one name with `input()`, clean it, and print the result.

help.concept: A helper module lets `main.py` stay short while another file handles one focused job.
help.hint_2: In `main.py`, import `clean_name`, call it with the raw input, and print the cleaned name.

try-using-imports-and-helper-files-check.starterCode:
raw_name = input()

# Import clean_name from tools.names.
# Print the cleaned name.

main_py.content:
from tools.names import clean_name

raw_name = input()
print(clean_name(raw_name))

try-using-imports-and-helper-files-check.solutionCode:
from tools.names import clean_name

raw_name = input()
print(clean_name(raw_name))

try-using-imports-and-helper-files-sketc.hint: Put `clean_name` in `tools/names.py`, then import it in `main.py`.

help.concept: This card is about moving the helper out of `main.py`, not keeping everything in one file.
help.hint_2: In `main.py`, use `from tools.names import clean_name`, then print `clean_name("  ava  ")`.

try-using-imports-and-helper-files-sketc.title: Create and import a badge helper
try-using-imports-and-helper-files-sketc.prompt:
Build this as a two-file program.

1. Create `tools/badges.py`.
2. In `tools/badges.py`, define `make_badge(name, role)`.
3. Return `ROLE badge: Name` with the role uppercased.
4. In `main.py`, import `make_badge`.

help.concept: The formatting helper belongs in its own module so `main.py` only connects the pieces.

try-using-imports-and-helper-files-sketc.starterCode:

# Create tools/badges.py with make_badge(name, role).
# Then import make_badge from tools.badges and print a student badge for Ava.

tools_badges_py.content:

try-using-imports-and-helper-files-sketc.title: Connect two helper modules
try-using-imports-and-helper-files-sketc.prompt:
Build this as a small multi-file program.

1. Create `tools/names.py` with `clean_name(text)`.
2. Create `tools/badges.py` with `make_badge(name, role)`.
3. In `main.py`, import both helpers.
4. Clean `"  mia  "`.
5. Build a mentor badge from the cleaned name and print it.
try-using-imports-and-helper-files-sketc.hint: Import both helpers in `main.py`, then clean the name before calling `make_badge`.

help.concept: This card practices the full pattern: helper files do the work, and `main.py` wires them together.
help.hint_1: Use `from tools.names import clean_name` and `from tools.badges import make_badge`.
help.hint_2: Clean `"  mia  "`, pass the result into `make_badge(..., "mentor")`, and print the badge.

try-using-imports-and-helper-files-sketc.starterCode:
# Create a folder named tools.
# Create tools/names.py with clean_name(text).
# Create tools/badges.py with make_badge(name, role).
# Then import both helpers, clean "  mia  ", and print a mentor badge.

main_py.content:
from tools.names import clean_name
from tools.badges import make_badge

name = clean_name("  mia  ")
print(make_badge(name, "mentor"))

tools_badges_py.content:
def make_badge(name, role):
    return f"{role.upper()} badge: {name}"

try-using-imports-and-helper-files-sketc.solutionCode:
from tools.names import clean_name
from tools.badges import make_badge

name = clean_name("  mia  ")
print(make_badge(name, "mentor"))

sk1.title: Your app can have more than one file
sk1.bodyMarkdown:
A Python project does not have to keep every function in `main.py`. When a program grows, it is cleaner to move focused functions into small module files.

For this lesson, imagine a tiny **Mission Control** app:

```text
main.py
tools/
  names.py
  badges.py
  reports.py
```

Each file has a job:

- `main.py` reads input and connects the pieces.
- `tools/names.py` cleans names.
- `tools/badges.py` builds badge text.
- `tools/reports.py` formats the final report.

The folder name and file name become the import path. A function inside `tools/names.py` can be imported like this:

```python
from tools.names import clean_name
```

Notice that the import uses `tools.names`, not `tools/names.py`. Python import paths use dots and leave off `.py`.

In the project, you will create module files in a folder and import your own functions into `main.py`.

sk2.title: Import from a file inside a folder
sk2.bodyMarkdown:
Suppose you create this file:

```text
tools/names.py
```

Inside `tools/names.py`, you can define a function:

```python
def clean_name(text):
    return text.strip().title()
```

Then `main.py` can import and use it:

```python
from tools.names import clean_name

raw_name = input()
name = clean_name(raw_name)
print(name)
```

Step by step:

1. Python looks inside the `tools` folder.
2. It finds the `names.py` module.
3. It imports the `clean_name` function.
4. `main.py` calls that function like any other function.

This keeps your main app clean. Instead of filling `main.py` with every detail, you give each module a focused job.

sk3.title: Build a small tool folder
sk3.bodyMarkdown:
A nice multi-file project is not just random files. Each module should have a clear purpose.

Example:

```text
tools/
  names.py     # clean and format names
  badges.py    # build badge labels
  reports.py   # format final messages
```

The files can work together through imports:

```python
from tools.names import clean_name
from tools.badges import make_badge
from tools.reports import mission_report

name = clean_name("  zoe  ")
badge = make_badge(name, "scout")
print(mission_report(badge, 18))
```

Output:

```python
Mission report: SCOUT badge: Zoe has 18 points
```

This is the main idea of the project: create small module files, put reusable functions inside them, import those functions into `main.py`, and connect them into one clean app.
